_meta.description: Strategies 1–5 with long definitions added (v1.3). This is chunk 01 of the full set.

speaking: Students will orally {function} {content_focus} using {supports} (e.g., {frames}), producing {product} that meets {criteria}.
Students will discuss {content_focus} in pairs, using {frames} to {function} and incorporate {target_vocab}.
listening: Students will comprehend {input_type} on {content_focus} and demonstrate understanding by {response_mode} using {supports}.
Students will follow {procedural_input} and {function} (e.g., sequence, identify) the steps for {content_focus}.
reading: Students will read {text_type} about {content_focus} and {function} (e.g., identify, infer) key ideas using {strategy} and {target_vocab}.
Students will use {strategy} (e.g., cognates, text features) to make meaning from {text_type} about {content_focus}.
writing: Students will write a {genre} to {purpose} about {content_focus}, using {frames}/{target_vocab} and {language_features}.
Students will revise a short paragraph to {function} (e.g., explain, justify) understanding of {content_focus} using {criteria}.

speaking+listening: In structured talk, students will {function} about {content_focus}, using {frames} while peers listen and {response_mode}.
reading+writing: Students will read {text_type} on {content_focus} and write a {genre} that {purpose}, using {target_vocab} and {criteria}.
all: Through {framework}, students will listen to {input_type}, discuss {content_focus}, read {text_type}, and write a {genre} that {purpose}, with {supports}.

strategies.core_principle: Leverage L1–L2 dictionaries to clarify new terms and support independent comprehension.
applicable_contexts: Content lessons with dense academic vocabulary
Writing tasks requiring precise word choice
implementation_steps: Model context-first inference, then confirm with a bilingual dictionary.
Embed routine look-ups in reading/writing; teach efficient dictionary skills.
Verify fit-in-context (e.g., reverse-translation or image check).
primary_benefits: Builds bilingual vocabulary and learner autonomy
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Purposeful use of L1–L2 dictionaries/glossaries to clarify meaning, verify usage, and build independent vocabulary learning habits.
definitions.long_en: Bilingual Dictionary and Glossary Use is a structured routine where students consult L1–L2 resources to confirm meaning, usage, and collocations of key terms while reading or writing. Instruction emphasizes context-first inference, then verification with a dictionary to avoid overreliance on literal translation. Teachers model efficient lookup behaviors (e.g., scanning multiple senses, checking part of speech, reverse-translation, and confirming with an image or example sentence). Students embed brief lookups during reading, note synonyms and cognates, and test fit by substituting the word back into the sentence. In writing, they use dictionaries to refine precision (e.g., academic versus everyday terms) and check morphology. This strategy increases comprehension of dense texts, develops metalinguistic awareness, and fosters learner autonomy. Use it in content areas with specialized vocabulary or when precision matters; avoid turning it into a stop-every-line routine that disrupts fluency. Set clear time limits, teach when to skip low-importance words, and always confirm that the selected term makes sense in context.
look_fors: Students infer from context, then verify with the dictionary
Teacher models checking part of speech and multiple senses
Reverse-translation or example sentence used to confirm fit
Brief, timed lookups that do not disrupt reading flow
non_examples: Choosing the first dictionary entry without checking context
Using dictionaries as a substitute for teaching word learning strategies

strategies.core_principle: Display key terms in English and L1 to make vocabulary constantly visible and meaningful.
implementation_steps: Co-create bilingual word cards with visuals; organize by theme or alphabet.
Routinely reference during instruction and practice via quick games.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Dual-language word walls and labels that pair key terms with visuals to make academic vocabulary continually visible and usable.
definitions.long_en: Bilingual Word Walls and Labels display high-utility academic and content-specific terms in English alongside students’ L1 equivalents, often with visuals, morphology cues, or example frames. Teachers co-create the wall with students, adding words during unit launches and revisiting it daily in mini-routines (quick reads, choral responses, sort-and-match, or ‘use-it-in-a-sentence’). Cards may be organized by theme, concept map, or part of speech; color-coding highlights cognates or affixes. During lessons, teachers point to the wall to prompt accurate retrieval, help students choose precise terms in speaking and writing, and reinforce spelling and pronunciation. Because the wall lives in the room, it supports independence, inclusion, and repeated exposure—key drivers of vocabulary growth. Use it in vocabulary-heavy units and literacy centers; keep it curated and legible. Avoid cluttered, static displays that are never referenced; the wall must be interactive and aligned to current learning goals to remain effective.
look_fors: Co-created bilingual cards with visuals or morphology cues
Daily reference during instruction and practice
Organized, current terms tied to the unit focus
Students using wall terms in speaking and writing
non_examples: A dense, outdated wall never referenced in lessons
Random long lists with no visuals or organization
Using only English labels in bilingual classrooms

strategies.core_principle: Teach learning strategies (metacognitive, cognitive, social) alongside content.
implementation_steps: Set content + strategy goals; model with think-alouds.
Guide practice (e.g., summarizing, graphic organizers) and reflect on transfer.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: A strategy-based framework that teaches metacognitive, cognitive, and social strategies alongside content to build academic language.
definitions.long_en: CALLA (Cognitive Academic Language Learning Approach) is a structured framework that integrates explicit teaching of learning strategies—metacognitive (plan, monitor, evaluate), cognitive (summarize, infer, organize), and social/affective—into content instruction. Teachers name the strategy purpose, model it with a think‑aloud tied to content objectives, and guide students in applying the strategy with supports (e.g., graphic organizers, sentence frames). Lessons end with reflection on how the strategy helped and when to transfer it to new tasks. Over time, students internalize a repertoire for approaching complex texts and problems while growing academic language functions. CALLA is well suited to upper-elementary and secondary classes, or any lesson demanding comprehension of dense input or multistep reasoning. It is not a grab-bag of tips; effectiveness depends on deliberate cycles of modeling, guided practice, application, and reflection. Avoid overloading students with many strategies at once—teach a small set deeply and spiral their use across units.
look_fors: Explicit naming of the strategy and its purpose
Teacher think‑aloud that models the strategy on content
Guided practice before independent application
Student reflection on when/how to use the strategy
non_examples: Unlabeled use of organizers without strategy language
Introducing several new strategies in a single lesson
Skipping reflection on transfer to future tasks

strategies.core_principle: Plan for simultaneous content objectives and language objectives with scaffolding.
implementation_steps: State content + language objectives; pre-teach key terms.
Use visuals/realia/organizers; build interactive tasks using target language.
primary_benefits: Promotes content mastery and language growth
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: An integrated approach that plans simultaneous content and language objectives with scaffolding in authentic subject lessons.
definitions.long_en: Content and Language Integrated Learning (CLIL) is an instructional approach where subject content (e.g., science, history) is taught through the target language, with explicit planning for language objectives and scaffolds. Teachers identify the disciplinary concepts and the language functions/forms needed to express them (e.g., compare, cause–effect, passive voice), pre-teach key vocabulary, and design tasks that require purposeful language use to solve problems or present findings. Lessons leverage visuals, realia, models, and structured interaction (think‑pair‑share, expert groups) to balance cognitive challenge and language support. Assessment addresses both content understanding and language growth. CLIL is effective in immersion and mainstream settings because it situates language learning in meaningful, knowledge-rich contexts. It should not be reduced to content coverage with incidental language; without planned objectives and scaffolding, learners may grasp ideas but lack the language to express them. Ensure every activity specifies the targeted language function and provides supports for success.
look_fors: Stated content and language objectives
Pre-teaching of key vocabulary and functions
Tasks that require producing target language for content goals
Use of visuals/realia and structured interaction
non_examples: Teaching subject content with no language objectives
Open discussion with high cognitive load but no scaffolds
Assessing only content, ignoring language evidence

strategies.core_principle: Make explicit links between cognates (e.g., informação–information) and teach false cognates.
implementation_steps: Chart L1–L2 cognates and revisit in units.
Design recognition and sorting tasks; teach common false pairs.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Explicit teaching of L1–L2 cognates and false cognates to accelerate vocabulary growth and metalinguistic insight.
definitions.long_en: Cognate Awareness makes cross-language connections visible by teaching students to recognize, verify, and use cognates (and avoid false cognates). Instruction highlights common roots, affixes, and sound–spelling patterns between L1 and English (e.g., informação–information, energia–energy), and builds routines for checking meaning in context. Students sort and chart cognate families, annotate texts to mark likely cognates, and practice using them in speaking and writing with attention to part of speech and morphology. Teachers also surface frequent false pairs (e.g., “pasta” vs. “folder”) to reduce misconceptions. This accelerates vocabulary acquisition in content reading, strengthens decoding of academic terms with Greco‑Latin roots, and deepens metalinguistic awareness. Use it to launch new units or when texts contain many technical terms; avoid assuming that all look‑alike words are cognates or focusing only on translation without contextual verification. Always pair cognate work with meaning-making activities so words are learned as concepts, not isolated items.
look_fors: Charts or organizers grouping cognate families
Students marking candidate cognates while reading
Attention to false cognates and context verification
Use of cognates in speaking/writing with correct form
non_examples: Treating all look‑alike words as true cognates
Isolated lists without application in reading/writing
